bank: Headline with more emotional or sensational wording gets more clicks than a neutral headline.
Headline that includes a number or list format gets more clicks than one without numbers.
Headline that poses a question or creates curiosity gets more clicks than a straightforward statement.
Headline that uses shorter, simpler words gets more clicks than one with complex vocabulary.
Headline that directly addresses the reader (using 'you' or 'your') gets more clicks than a headline without direct address.
Headline that includes a time reference (e.g., 'today', 'now', 'this week') gets more clicks than one without a time reference.
Headline that uses active voice gets more clicks than a headline written in passive voice.
Headline that mentions a well-known person or entity gets more clicks than one that does not.
Headline that emphasizes exclusivity or rarity (e.g., 'only', 'exclusive', 'never seen before') gets more clicks than one without such emphasis.
Headline that uses strong verbs or action words gets more clicks than one with weak or generic verbs.
Headline that creates a sense of urgency or scarcity (e.g., 'limited time', 'ending soon') gets more clicks than one without urgency.
Headline that includes a surprising or counterintuitive claim gets more clicks than one with an expected statement.
Headline that uses humor or wordplay gets more clicks than a straightforward or serious headline.
Headline that highlights personal benefit or practical value to the reader gets more clicks than one without clear benefits.
Headline that incorporates local or community-specific references gets more clicks than a generic or global headline.
